title: Bread sauce
subtitle: Warm spiced milk thickened with white breadcrumbs.
excerpt: Whole milk infused with onion, cloves, and bay leaves, then stirred with white breadcrumbs until thick and creamy. Finished with butter and a grating of fresh nutmeg. The oldest English sauce, served with roast chicken and turkey.
sourceNotes: Bread sauce is one of the oldest sauces in the English culinary repertoire, appearing in recipe collections from the sixteenth and seventeenth centuries. It predates flour-thickened sauces and represents an older form of cookery where stale bread was used to thicken and body liquids. It has retained its association with roast poultry, particularly chicken and turkey, more strongly than almost any other medieval-derived preparation in British cooking.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Make up to two hours ahead and keep warm over a very low heat, adding a splash of milk if it thickens.

content.text: The onion, cloves, and bay leaves are removed before the breadcrumbs go in: they are there only to flavour the milk. Give the infusion time. Ten minutes of simmering milk with these aromatics produces a sauce that smells and tastes unmistakably of bread sauce; five minutes produces milk with a faint suggestion of cloves.
Use day-old white bread with the crusts removed and blitzed or grated to crumbs. Fresh bread makes the sauce gluey; stale crumbs are fluffier and absorb the milk more evenly.
A medium saucepan. No other equipment needed.

Put {{whole-milk}} of milk in a saucepan with the onion halves, cloves pressed into the cut face of the onion, and bay leaves. Bring to a gentle simmer over a medium-low heat. Simmer for 10 minutes to infuse, then remove and discard the onion, cloves, and bay leaves.

Stir {{breadcrumbs-dried}} of breadcrumbs into the hot infused milk. Cook over a low heat, stirring constantly, for 5 minutes until the sauce is thick and creamy. Add {{unsalted-butter}} of butter and stir until melted. Grate over {{nutmeg-whole}} of nutmeg. Season with {{sea-salt-fine}} of salt. Stir in the double cream if using. Serve hot.

items.cause: Breadcrumbs added too fast or to milk that was not hot enough.
items.fix: Stir vigorously. Lumpy bread sauce can be whisked smooth or pushed through a sieve.

items.cause: Breadcrumbs absorbed more milk than expected.
items.fix: Add a splash of hot milk and stir until the right consistency. Bread sauce thickens quickly as it cools.
Simmer the aromatics for a full 10 minutes. Cloves and bay need time to release flavour into milk.

items.symptom: Sauce has a skin on top
items.fix: Stir in a small knob of butter, which prevents a skin forming. Cover with a lid or surface-contact cling film.

content.text: Bread sauce is one of very few sauces in British cookery that predate the flour-roux tradition brought over from France in the seventeenth and eighteenth centuries. It belongs to an older English cookery where stale bread was a thickener of choice for soups, sauces, and puddings. That it has survived in association with roast chicken and turkey for over four centuries speaks to how well it works: the mild, spiced creaminess against the richness of poultry is a combination that has not needed improving.
